transcript.text: Thank you.
Hi, Clarissa.
Hi, Bridget.
How are you?
I'm good.
How are you?
I'm great.
Before we start, no worries.
Before we start, I just want to apologize.
I tried myself because I had an unpredicted trip to make today, so I am at the airport right now, but I hope this doesn't impact negatively in our conversation.
So just let me know if the noise is too much, because I can't deal with that.
No worries, where are you going?
I actually came to Belo Horizonte, which is my city, but I live in Brasília.
So I just came for like an hour meeting, and I'm just returning to my city right now.
Wow.
Okay, I'm looking on a map.
I have friends, I always pronounce it wrong.
Is it Belo Horizonte or something?
Horizonte?
Yeah, that's right.
Belo Horizonte.
Yeah.
Belo Horizonte.
Yeah.
Okay, and you live in Brasília?
Yeah, now I live in Brasília for, I think, almost eight years.
Oh, that's kind of a far flight, like to...
Is that a couple hours?
It is actually like 15 minutes only.
Nice.
Yeah, that's pretty close.
Yeah, cool.
Well, I'm excited to meet you.
I listened to your interview already with Marilia, so I have a decent amount of context.
But I'll tell you about me and the company and the role from my perspective first.
So I joined on February 1st, pretty much.
So I guess I'm three months in, and I'm running all operations right now.
So like people ops, recruiting, finance, IT, legal, kind of like, you know, back office stuff.
And then also obviously hiring for like customer ops slash ops generalists to help around the org.
And so my previous, in my previous company, I was running like biz ops team, which was a little bit more rev.-bye.
Bye
Like go-to-market focused.
Like we thought a lot about like, yeah, more like on the revenue side of operations, like dashboarding and pulling like monthly reports and metrics on the pipeline and things like that.
But also did a lot of different like special projects within the orgs.
Oh, so anyway, so that's my background.
And the company, just to give you like, it's very new for me, this business model.
So I'll try to explain it as best I can.
But essentially it's today, it looks like an agency to the customers.
And so they're buying services from us and we're doing whatever we need to do behind the scenes to like, they're buying an outcome, like they want content.
Or maybe they're buying like programmatic SEO.
Like they just want us to like, do the work for them.
It's really like an agency interaction.
But what we're doing.
Behind the scenes really is like building AI workflows to be able to produce a lot more content than like a typical person could actually do.
And so we're building these workflows and they're still, you know, we're a new company.
Like we've only really been operating since like September.
So it's a bit nascent still.
But the goal eventually is to be able to like actually build like a platform on top of all these workflows.

So we can transition to be more of like a tech company where the customer could either operate them themselves or at least log into something where they can see what they're doing and the results and all of that.
So we will over the next few months or a year, like sort of transition from like more of like a traditional agency model to being more of like a technology company.
And so, so today the way the team is set up is like in two orgs we have.
We call them build and deliver.
And deliver is like all the folks working on like client delivery work.
And that's like the majority of the organization today, just because we have a lot of customers and we're producing a lot of content.
So it's just a lot of people.
And then we have the build team, which is like all of the engineers and my team, essentially like building the product and running the business.
And it's like a smaller core group.
So there's like the other thing I'll explain context for this role is how to think about it is like there's sort of two interactions with product to consider.
There's like what we produce and deliver to the customer.
So that's like the one that, you know, the customer interacting with our work.
But then on the other side, there's like our team using AI workflows, like prompting, reprompting, editing, giving.

Back to the engineering team, hey, this one is  or doesn't work, or this is like not expected outcomes, or can you fix this, improve it, so that over time the AI is better and better and better and better and better.
So that's like our team learning how to use the AI and the workflows.
So there's like this other product side of things, which is like our team using our product, and then there's like whatever the outcome is delivered to the customer.
So there's almost like two levels there that we just need to take into consideration.
And like the point of the team using the AI is so that we can, the end goal is like deliver for our customers, obviously.
So like taking that into consideration and how do we show value to customers and like drilling that down to like what are people doing every day?
Where are they spending time?
Where are they getting blocked?
How can we make everybody's lives easier and more efficient?
So we can deliver more and more value.
So

I know I'm rambling for a bit, but I hope that gives context because it's a bit different than, like, either a traditional agency or a traditional, like, SaaS company where, you know, day one they signed a contract and you're like, here's the platform that's going to do work for you.
It's in the middle somewhere.
So that's been new for me and most people.
Like, I don't know that we have anybody who's worked in that sort of a company before.

But that's why, like, for this role, it's really important for someone to be, like, able to think first principles sort of about, like, what we're trying to achieve and then how to best break it down into, like, little bite-sized, like, operational tactics to get there.
Like, you know, break it down into, like, improve here, here, here, here, and stuff like that.
So I will pause.
Any initial questions on that?
Like, I just wanted to give you the context so when I ask you questions about your experience, you can draw parallels to what we're doing here.
Sure, it was pretty clear.
I don't have any specific question right now.
I think we can jump into your own questions because then I can connect because it seems really similar to the operations that we had at Rock Content when I joined there.
But let's go for your questions first.
That's the one I want to hear about.
Tell me about your role at Rock Content.
Maybe how it changed over time as well.
It looked like it was growing and things.
So I'm curious about your core responsibilities and how it changed.
Sure.
Yeah.
I joined Rock Content actually to build something pretty similar because Rock Content was also born as a company, very services company.
So they were delivering lots of service to the customer.
So it's like an agent as well.
So we were producing content in high scale, but using freelancers at that time.
So when I joined Rock,
More tech, into a more tech business.
We were actually still very supported by services.
And our technology also was very service needed because the platform itself wasn't so intuitive.
The user didn't have the best experience.

So we were the one, actually my team at that time, were the ones performing the services in our platform without any help of AI, which I know I'm saying a lot about AI because I think this was the hugest mistake that we had at Rock at that time.
Because we are like struggling with the technology and we were not improving so fast as we could to change the service needs into technology.
Because then I was the responsible for not just working with the new GTM that we were making, because once again, Rock was born as a service company, and we were trying to be a technology company.
So we revamped all the GTM that we had at that time.

And also, we needed to review all the offerings, all the operational side, because we didn't have the support of many tools for delivering, for monitoring the services that we were delivering, for monitoring projects.
It wasn't like a reality at Rock, that we would work such as project managers.
Just so I understand the product, it's like, would the brand sign a contract with you, with Rock, and they get access to a platform where...
It's a marketplace for freelancers to write for them, and they're kind of, like, selecting the free...
So it's kind of, like, Upwork-ish?
Yeah, yeah, kind of.
Actually, they were trying to build, like, an Upwork or even an Uber for content production.
So the idea was to be a company which delivered the technology for connecting freelancers and companies.
However, the reality was that the customers were very dependent on the services that we were delivering because we were actually...
Our team was mainly composed by experts on marketing strategies, SEO, and those things.
So we couldn't rely that much on freelancers to deliver the quality of work that we were expecting to...
I think...
I

So the customer would buy some, like the contract, or like the agreement with you would provide both strategy from someone on the rock team directly, as well as access to the freelancers to execute the actual writing itself?
Yeah, that's totally right.
Okay.
The team that was actually performing freelancers' operations was an internal team.
So the customer actually dealt with our team at Raw Content and not with the platform itself.
So that's why that was the mindset change that we were trying to make with a new GTM to have the customer buying the technology and not the services.
Yeah, buy the marketplace.
see.
And so what, what, where did you spend most of your time?

Most of my time on the first year and a half maybe was mostly reviewing the whole operations because we need to have scalability on the delivery and we need to revamp the way we were serving the customers.
So most of my time was actually reviewing the operations itself, understanding what does the client want and how could we manage all the needs of the customers with a more low-touch approach.
So not having one person.
Actually, we used to have like three different teams interacting with the customer for the same need, for delivering only one service.
So we used to have, for example, the customer success team, we used to have the managed services team, and also an analyst for the marketplace.

So I spent most of my time revamping these whole operations, making it more seamless and more clean to avoid friction on the customer experience itself, but also to help the operations to be scalable.
How did you go about it?
I'm curious how you even approached that from day one.
I used to say to my manager that it wasn't that easy, because it seems that I joined the company to make some layoffs, because I needed to terminate many contracts with our internal team.
But I think the way I deal with it since day one was mainly understanding how could we actually improve the experience from...
Both sides, not just for raw content, but also for customers, because we used to walk like in silos, as we had so many people interacting and dealing and serving the customer.
This generated lots of problems to the service delivery itself.
So I tried to be very close to the operations.

I tried to connect a lot with not just the customer org, which I was part of, but also with the product team, because I needed to understand how could we bring some features and some solutions into the technology to avoid those silos to happen.
So that was mostly how I dealt with the situation.
Again, I was pretty close to the team that was actually delivering the services, understanding their needs, and also connecting with other stakeholders too.
To bring root cause solutions and also to design a different model of operating itself.
What was the biggest friction, I guess?
I guess, was there anything that was like a recurring theme that was the main problem, you thought, I guess?
I think part of our huge pain point was actually under the manager services side.
Because we used to have a content strategist and a managing editor at the same time.
One of them was making the strategy and the other one was mainly providing the content itself, reviewing what the freelancers were producing.
Yeah, so...
Those were the main frictions I found, because it seems like a double work done by different people, and they were not that much aligned among each other.
The work that the strategists were doing, when it arrived into the managing editor team, they were like, it doesn't connect.
It seems that the managing editors understand more from the client's needs than the content strategy.
So this was the main friction that I found out.
That was also one of the changes that we made, because we then shortened the team.
We made just one person dealing with the whole strategy, and reviewing also, which we used to call, make the occurrence for the content that...
Plus produce it so that we could use more the freelancers, the freelancer services and have a more strategic approach from the services that we were providing inside raw content.
Okay, so it sounds like you used to have three levels, which is like the managing editor doing strategy, the content strategist responsible for the output, but they were also just using freelancers.
So there were three levels, and then you shrunk it to two, where there was just the managing editor and then the freelancers?
Yeah, that's totally right.
What were the downsides of that approach?
I know you went with that eventually, but what were the hesitations of going that way?
First of all, I believe that the team was struggling with a huge workload to deal with, so they were...
Like, this seems too much for myself.
So once they used to take care of, for example, 10 to 15 projects, and then they were requesting to actually deal with less than five projects at that time.
Yeah, because you're taking away a whole person.
Yeah, yeah.

So this was a huge trade-off we need to make because although, yeah, they were right that they were doing, like, more, but the idea was for them to prepare the freelancers' base to be able to deal with the needs that we were delivering because they were very operational at that time.
So they weren't strategic.
And the best value that we were delivering...
To the customer was actually the strategic part that was the expertise that Brock had at that time.
So I think it was very challenging to deal with that.

I needed them to be very close to the product team, to have them onboarded and to make them understand that the feature that I was asking, they were mainly to be able to have this scalability and to be more strategic on delivering services on the best way.
Because otherwise, actually, our P&L and our revenues were not closing because we didn't, our operations was very expensive.
Because we had many, many people to work just in one specific project.
So it was very challenging.

Especially in terms of people, because they were used to a type of working, they were used to a type of processes, but to be honest, I think at the end, they kind of understood all the movements that we made, and they were actually saying that it made total sense to be a more project-oriented service delivery.

And so you were able to produce the same amount of content and same output, even by removing that one layer completely, because then the strategist was directly talking to and passing context to freelancers so they could be more productive?
Is that kind of...
Yeah, that's right.
Because then when I joined and when we had the managing editor and the content strategist, then it was like the strategist said something, they planned something.
The managing editors understood what the strategy was planning.
They were the ones to be meeting the requests to the freelancers and the freelancers were delivering.
The managing editor was reviewing all the deliveries from the freelancers and they were the ones delivering to the customer.
But the strategy was the responsible for dealing with the results.
So if, for example, they were not ranking anymore at Google, if they were not having the same amount of visits in their website, the responsible was actually the strategy.
So you understood properly because the main point is that.
By removing this role specifically, we were more straight and we were more connected with customer and freelancer delivering, customer needs and freelancer delivering.
Because if the freelancer had any issue, any doubt, they would connect directly with the strategist and not having the managing editor in the middle, maybe providing their own understanding.
Yeah.
So it's funny because we have that set up today.
Exactly.
We have three layers.
And I mean, we always debate what's a good set up.
I think we're lucky in that we don't have to worry about margins.
I could see if you really needed to improve your cost structure and margins that you would...
I don't know that we're, we don't feel that pain that we need to improve the margins.
part of it's like, I think our concern with cutting out a layer is that there's just such a...
Fathom between like the level of the strategy person and the level of the freelancer.
And then just we wouldn't I think I would be worried that our strategists would spend too much time editing and checking the quality of freelancers work instead of spending time on strategy.
So I don't know.
What do you think of that?
Those would be my concerns would be like the middle layer.
It's hopefully making the strategist's life easier, but I totally understand that like it just gets lost.
The context gets lost in the layers and you just know it's like back and forth because they didn't understand what they're even supposed to do.
I don't know.
Yeah, yeah.
I think I think what is what makes a huge difference from what it seems that growthx is building and from what happening what from what happened actually.
With raw content at that time was that, first of all, we were not counting on technology that much.
So we were counting on people itself.
And then we needed to deal with ego as well.
So we need to deal with accountability that maybe we had lots of gaps between the strategist accountability and the managing editor accountability as well.
So it was easier for them to just say, no, but I planned something and the managing editor didn't produce what I just planned.
And then, you know, like having those connections and not having the technology itself to be a huge part of all those things.
This was very painful for us at Raw Content, which I believe it's different from what you guys are building at GrowthX, because you are actually...
Trying to build a technology based on the knowledge and all the expertise of those teams.
And they are helping, actually, to bring strategic insights, which is kind of different from what we faced at ROC.
That's mainly the difference I see.

But I agree with you because, again, when we made this change, I then needed to deal with the strategies saying, like, I cannot deliver so many results as I was delivering because I do not have time to spend on the strategy itself.
But, again, we had the strategy who requested the plan for the freelancer.
And then we had...
We had another person in the team, which was the managing editor, who requested the content production for the freelancers.
So there was lots of gaps in this process.
And we were not counting.
Oh, sorry.
No, and we were not just counting with technology.
So I think this is mainly the point that we got.
What kind of, I don't know if you used any automations or systems, like how did, how do you, how do you think about accountability from operations side of things?
I think about this a lot too, just even my team and stuff.
Like, yeah, how did you think about that?
Because I could see that that if there was some way to take the accountability babysitting off the strategist, then they feel better that they don't have to just babysit all day.
And heard, you know, make sure all these random people are doing all these.
Little tasks.
Did you have ways to automate that for yourself or the team?
I've tried, actually.
When I started managing both teams, I implemented a master tracker, which was spreadsheets connected with all the customer spreadsheets and to bring an overview of all the projects.

So I tried to build not just this master tracker to have all the projects in one only view, but also to provide some KPIs and to understand where were the pain points like, are we taking too much to deliver to the customer or are we taking too much to actually receive the customer feedback and to receive the customer approval?
But then I've implemented this as a way of trying...
Trying to connect all the teams and trying to have their own accountability for their own responsibilities.
But also, I used to, and we tried actually, to revamp HubSpot operations because we had HubSpot as a tool for CRM, but this tool was mainly used by the sales team and the customer success team.
They were not being used by the operations team.
So I also tried to build something that connects the other areas as well.
But then this is very challenging.
think automating this process without having properly investments on technology, we can deal with that, but it's very challenging.
Even with the technology itself, I'm feeling...

I'm this right now at JustFi, the company that I'm working with, because we are also implementing HubSpot to have this overview of the customer journey as a whole, and also to connect the initiatives from marketing, from sales, and from the services team.
But there are ways of trying to do that.
There are best platforms to use, and there are ways to deliver this in a more house-making way, you know?
But then at Rock, I tried to build this master tracker, and I have connected all the projects.
I have actually developed a unique template for every project to be managed through.
So there was one Google Sheet that was a template, and every...
Everybody should use the same template for each project, for each customer.
And through those specific spreadsheets for each customer, I used to have a big one that made all the connections with Vlookup and with some formulas to reveal everything that was happening.
And how do you make sure people are updating templates?
Because I find that sometimes you're like, hey, it looks like you haven't done anything because there's no, you didn't put the due date or you didn't check done.
Like, were you basically having to go through everybody's all the time?
Was that part of your role to like check?
Hey, I actually had some supervisors doing that.
But on a monthly basis, mostly, I used to review everything and I used...
To go and to ask for the supervisors and to understand why we didn't have that or that information.
And also, for sure, people were also asking me, like, oh, I know your team didn't provide this information for this project.
The customer is complaining, so help me to find out what's going on.
But mostly, I used to have this monthly reviewing meeting with the team to actually make sure that things were happening on the proper way.
What's the role of a supervisor?
Are they managing people?
Or are they more just, like, checking that things are being done?
They were mostly managing people.
But not the strategist?
was separate?
No, because, actually, they were managing the strategists and the managing editor.
So we used to have to...
Different teams with two different leaderships under my own leadership.
So, again, it was a very messy operation.
Like, we had many people working for the same purpose and for the same customer.
But, yeah, they were mainly doing more like people management.
And I was trying to implement the processes to make sure that they were following the processes that we had a standard.
So it was kind of this.
I don't know if it was clear enough for you, but it was kind of this type of operation that we had.
Yeah.
No, no.
It definitely, I understand why there was like organizational bloat and redundancy in tasks.
And then it makes accountability even harder because it's like, that's not my job to do that.
And, yeah.
Okay.
Well, thank you for going through all that.
It super helpful.
Because you have such a similar experience.
It's in that company.
It helps a lot draw the parallels.
What questions can I answer for you?
We have until 11.15.
Yeah, what questions can I answer for you?
And if you have to go for your flight, just let me know.
No, that's totally okay.
I still have lots of time in here.
But I was curious because you mentioned that you guys were actually building the technology like you are now a very service company, delivering and whatever the customer wants, just like an agent.
And I was curious if the AI intelligence, the AI technology that you guys are building is owned by growthx, first of all.
And also, in what level are you guys right now in terms of having, at least, part of the product ready?
For the market, or you guys believe that this will take like a year yet to be ready for the market itself?

So the workflows exist today, and basically like our team, and it's kind of like, I think it would be your freelancer level, we have a bunch of like writers and SEO strategists who are using the workflows to produce, like if we're just talking about like editorial, they're using it to like produce blog posts and content.
So they're like, using our workflows and prompting them.
And we have a bunch of templates and things and they're able to like, run the AI to help them, you know, get most of the way there.

Of course, like there's a lot of human in the, there's always going to be a human in the loop there, like making sure the quality is there, like the tone is right, it's not hallucinating and making up fake things.
And, you know, it's the quality check, like, our customers are hiring us to do
Something that we should be able to do better than they would about, like, write better content than they would about their own company.
So it needs to have, like, really thoughtful research and, like, make sure we know, you know, it has to be very legit.
So the team's using that today.
But, like, as I said, it's, like, behind this hood.
Like, the customer doesn't see that, the workflow is being run.
We could eventually.
We're still deciding, like, monetization of the business.
But, like, there's no reason why we couldn't offer parts of those workflows as, like, standalone products even.
Like, for example, there's, like, a fact checker workflow where you can have it review the content and make sure it's factual.
And, like, that could, someone might buy that as a standalone thing.
So maybe they would go into our UI, like, our product and buy that as a standalone.
Or maybe we have like a competitive research workflow that like runs a bunch of competitive info research on your on the competitors, like produces a bunch of information.
And like, here's where your strengths, weaknesses, here's how you compare it, blah, blah.
Like that could be sold as a standalone product, too.
So, I mean, this is all very early, like we're deciding what to do with it.
But those are some examples.
I think the first thing we'll do is just like, probably make some kind of like platform where people can see even just like, how many contents or how many how many pieces of content do we deliver?
Maybe the rankings.
So maybe it's there's some dashboards of like, you know, in the last month, we've we've delivered this many articles.
Here's what your rankings is like.
Just like something to make like feel stickier, I think with services because everything's behind the scenes.
I think the customer doesn't value as much all of the work you're doing.
And so some way to show them, like through a product experience, like here's everything we've been doing.
That would be like V1, but then like V2 could be that we sell different components.
mean, that's possible too.
So it's early, but I'd say that's where we're at.
Oh, great.
It's pretty clear.

And yeah, I think this is the most challenge, at least at Rock, we had, which was providing the customer the knowledge of what we deliver and how does our deliver us helping them in their business, in their results.
So, yeah.
do you show it?
Again, throughout this master tracker that I had, and also our strategy used to make like the...

They used to have the Google Analytics access from our customers, and then they used to know which content has ranked better, and they were trying to provide knowledge throughout this type of analysis.
So it was a very human-touch analysis.
It was pretty manual, pretty, pretty manual.
So we struggled a lot to provide this.
Show it in like a week, in like a call.
Yeah, we used to have a QBR, and we used to present the results through all the QBR and showing them what we delivered, which was the best content that we were providing.
So we also helped the customer to understand what type of topic their customer were looking for.
So, yeah, but it was very, it was one of the main, the...

The main frictions that we found out about not having any specific person to go deeper into those analyses, because we were depending only on humans, and we were not being able to provide this as fast as we wanted, and even without as many detail as we wanted, as we didn't have time to go deeper on that.

So again, that is why I believe that the technology that you guys are trying to build is the huge difference and the huge, like, this will make all, actually, the difference from what Brock was trying to build, and we were far away from what you guys are right now.
So yeah, I think that this is one huge advantage, for sure.
Totally.
Agreed.
What else can I add?
Answer for you.
No, I think I'm okay.
I have talked also on the previous interview, and I have clarified all the questions that I had.
Awesome.
I think that's mostly it.
Perfect.
Okay, well, what I'm going to do is I'm going to, like, I was recording this, hope that's fine.

I'm going to send it to one of my colleagues who's helping me think about this role, and he would do the next interview screen as well, so I'll just see what he thinks, and then we'll probably move pretty quickly just because we have at least one other person who's a little bit further ahead in the process, so I'll get back to you today for sure on the next steps, and then I'll just, like, give a sense of timing as well.
But you mentioned a vacation.
Can you remind me when that is because I don't want to?
Yeah, actually, I don't have a vacation, but next week I will be out on a work trip, so I will be...
Focused in person in our office, which might be a little bit more difficult to find time to connect with you guys.
But I can deal with it.
I can manage this if needed.
So this is just a disclaimer about it.
I will keep you posted for sure.
Then at least we'll be able to get back to you today about moving forward and what the next step will be.
And we can try to either expedite or work around that a little bit.
But I'm like, there'll be one more.
The process is like one call with Jason who's next.
And then and then we do like a little take home assignment like you don't expect to spend a ton of time on it.
It's more like hypothetically, how would you it's essentially going to be like if you were to start this role, like how would you break it down into what you would even think about first?
And you're not expected to have like answers, obviously, because you have no context, but it's just to see how you think about things.
And it's a very interactive session.
So you'll like do a little bit of homework.
And then we'll come in and we'll all just like go through it together and think out loud.
So that's it.
But I understand that that one requires a bit of homework time and then two obviously requires like some meetings.
So we'll work.
We'll figure it out.
OK, and I can manage it also.
So just let me know.
Thank you very much for your time, Bridget.
Yeah, thank you.
It's great to meet you.
Have a safe flight.
Thank you.
I'm sorry again for this, but I hope it didn't impact.
I didn't hear a thing.
was perfect.
Don't worry.
OK, thank you very much.
Have a great afternoon there.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss Clarissa Castanha's experience and potential fit for an operations role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX is transitioning from an agency model to a tech company, building AI workflows for content production](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=381.0)
  - [Clarissa has relevant experience from Rock Content, where she streamlined operations in a similar agency-to-tech transition](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=695.0)
  - [GrowthX's use of AI technology sets it apart from Clarissa's previous experience, potentially offering better scalability](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=1740.0)
  - [Next steps include a follow-up interview with Jason and a take-home assignment to assess Clarissa's approach to the role](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2776.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's Business Model and Transition

  - [Currently operates like an agency, delivering content and SEO services to customers](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=389.0)
  - [Building AI workflows behind the scenes to increase content production capacity](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [Goal: Transition to a tech company with a platform for customers to access workflows](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=441.0)
  - [Two-sided product interaction:](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=508.0)
    1.  Customer-facing: Delivered content and outcomes
    2.  Internal: Team using AI workflows to produce content efficiently

### Clarissa's Experience at Rock Content

  - [Joined to help transition from services to tech-focused business](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=718.0)
  - [Revamped operations and GTM strategy](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=857.0)
  - [Streamlined team structure from 3 levels (strategist, managing editor, freelancer) to 2 (strategist, freelancer)](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=1110.0)
  - [Challenges:](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=1227.0)
      - [Increased workload for remaining team members](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=1110.0)
      - [Balancing strategic work with operational tasks](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=1021.0)
      - [Lack of robust technology support](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=1926.0)

### Operational Improvements and Challenges

  - [Implemented master tracker using spreadsheets to monitor projects and KPIs](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=1926.0)
  - [Attempted to revamp HubSpot operations for better cross-team connectivity](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=1926.0)
  - [Struggled with manual processes and human-dependent analyses](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2601.0)
  - [Monthly review meetings to ensure adherence to processes and standards](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2151.0)

### GrowthX's Current State and Future Plans

  - [AI workflows currently used internally by writers and SEO strategists](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2349.0)
  - [Considering standalone products from existing workflows (e.g., fact-checker, competitive research)](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2436.0)
  - [Near-term goal: Create a customer-facing platform to showcase work and results](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2488.0)
  - [Long-term potential: Transition to a SaaS model with customer-operated workflows](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2532.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Bridget to review the interview recording with a colleague](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2716.0)
  - [Clarissa to be informed about moving forward in the process today](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2776.0)
  - [Next interview to be scheduled with Jason](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2789.0)
  - [Take-home assignment to be given, focusing on Clarissa's approach to the role](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2798.0)
  - [Work around Clarissa's upcoming work trip to schedule further interviews](https://fathom.video/share/b8ER7y8XQFDiMUwQ8-xStdQQttj4te7x?tab=summary&timestamp=2834.0)
